rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ERNEST V. RHINE TRUST
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Pending before the court is Respondent's Motion for Summary Judgment or in the Alternative, Motion for Summary Adjudication to the Second Amended Petition to Contest the Validity of the Challenged First and Second Sets of Amendments to the Ernest V. Rhine Declaration of Trust, UDT April 9, 2000, on the Grounds of Undue Influence, filed in In The Ernest V. Rhine Trust (PTR-18-301983). The pleadings define the issues to be considered on a motion for summary judgment. (Sadlier v. Superior Court (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d 1050, 1055; Benedek v. PLC Santa Monica, LLC (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 1351, 1355-56). The Second Amended Petition's claims and causes of action include: (1) that the First Amendment to the Trust (September 14, 2004) was procured through undue influence, as defined in Probate Code section 86 and Welf. & Inst Code section 15610.70; and (2) that the Second Amendment to the Trust (March 19, 2005) was procured through undue influence, as defined in Probate Code section 86 and Welf. & Inst Code section 15610.70. Respondent's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Respondent's Motion for Summary Adjudication in the alternative ("MSA") is also DENIED. As explained below, triable issues of fact exist as to one or more causes of action and, therefore, Respondent is not entitled to judgment as a matter of law. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 437c(c).) The Court GRANTS Respondent's request for judicial notice. Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) The Court GRANTS Petitioner's request for judicial notice. Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) The Court OVERRULES both Petitioner's and Respondent's evidentiary objections. A party moving for summary judgment bears the "initial burden of production to make a prima facie showing of the nonexistence of any triable issue of material fact." (Falcon v. Long Beach Genetics, Inc. (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 1263, 1271). "A defendant moving for summary judgment is entitled to summary judgment if he or she either conclusively negates an element of the plaintiff's cause of action, or shows that the plaintiff cannot establish at least one element of the cause of action." (Teselle v. McLoughlin (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 156, 176, citing Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 853.) To satisfy its initial burden, a defendant must "present evidence ... and not simply point out that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." [Citation.] The defendant may satisfy this requirement in one of two ways: First, it may "present evidence that conclusively negates an element of the plaintiff's cause of action." [Citation.] In the alternative, defendant "may ... present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence-as through admissions by the plaintiff following extensive discovery to the effect that he has discovered nothing." [Citation]. Hypertouch, Inc. v. ValueClick, Inc. (2011) 192 Cal. App. 4th 805, 838. [Part 1 of 4]

Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ERNEST V. RHINE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] Even assuming the disputed issue of material fact regarding active procurement alone was not enough to establish undue influence in procurement of Decedent's testamentary documents, it is not presented alone. Petitioner has established factual disputes regarding events occurring before and after the testamentary instruments were procured. The allowable inferences arising from those facts include Decedent's vulnerability, Respondent's awareness of Decedent's vulnerability, Decedent's reliance on Respondent, Respondent's position of authority over Decedent's affairs as attorney in fact and trustee, and Respondent's interest as a beneficiary of the challenged testamentary documents. A reasonable trier of fact could conclude that the facts presented in this record support a finding that Respondent was involved in procuring the 2004 and 2005 Trust amendments and the power and control she exercised amounted to undue influence over the testamentary act. A trier of fact could also consider the facts and conclude that they do not support a finding of undue influence. This is the nature of disputes of material fact. Thus, these claims cannot be summarily adjudicated in favor of Respondent. The Motion also asserts that Petitioner lacks standing to contest the Trust's validity, or would lack standing if the challenge to the 2004 Trust amendment fails. The undisputed evidence presented in the Motion demonstrates that Petitioner is not named as a beneficiary in any of the challenged instruments or in the decedent's earlier estate planning documents executed in 1998 and 2000, before Decedent and Petitioner met. However, Respondent has not met her burden to present evidence that Petitioner lacks standing to challenge the instruments otherwise. The argument that Petitioner will lack standing to challenge the 2005 amendment if she is unsuccessful in challenging the 2004 amendment depends on an event which has not yet occurred and a legal determination that has not yet been made. Currently, the Court has already ruled in the November 14, 2018 Order After Hearing overruling Respondent's Demurrer to the Second Amended Petition (at 2:17-22), and in the June 14, 2019 Order Denying Kimberly Rhine Gagosian's Motion for Summary Judgment or in the Alternative Summary Adjudication (at 2:4-4:22), that Petitioner is an "interested person" under Probate Code section 48 in these proceedings. Respondent's standing argument fails. Finally, the Motion seeks summary adjudication of claims arising from post-execution facts. The motion does not specify what part of the Second Amended Petition Respondent is arguing are post-execution claims that ought to be summarily adjudicated. Paragraphs 10 and 11 specifically regard the related 2017 unlawful detainer action between the parties, paragraph 28 lists when Probate Code section 16061.7 Notice was served after Decedent's death. Other paragraphs are generally about parties' alleged interactions post-execution and Respondent's alleged continuing influence over Decedent (Paragraphs 31, 32, 55, 56, 57, 58, 60, 62, 64-71, 75, 76, 79). These paragraphs do not assemble into a separate cause of action, but do attempt to bolster the Petition's undue influence claims against Respondent regarding the 2004 and 2005 Trust amendments. A motion for summary adjudication shall be granted only if it completely disposes of a cause of action, an affirmative defense, a claim for damages, or an issue of duty (CCP section 437c(f)(1)). Removing these paragraphs from the Second Amended Petition would not completely dispose of either a cause of action, an affirmative defense, a claim for damages, or an issue of duty. Accordingly, summary adjudication is denied. [Part 3 of 4]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ERNEST V. RHINE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Summary Judgment (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] "The party opposing the summary judgment must make an independent showing by a proper declaration or by reference to a deposition or another discovery product that there is sufficient proof of the matters alleged to raise a triable question of fact if the moving party's evidence, standing alone, is sufficient to entitle the party to judgment. [Citations.] To avoid summary judgment, admissible evidence presented to the trial court, not merely claims or theories, must reveal a triable, material factual issue. [Citation.]" (Wiz Technology, Inc. v. Coopers & Lybrand (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 1, 10-11.) This burden shifting under the summary judgment statute between a defendant moving for summary judgment and a party opposing the motion for summary judgment differs from the general burden shifting schema found at trial. In deciding a motion for summary judgment or summary adjudication, the court must refrain from weighing the evidence, assessing the credibility of witnesses, or making any determination of fact. (Weiss v People ex rel Dep't of Transp. (2020) 9 Cal.5th 840, 864; Sandell v Taylor-Listug, Inc. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 297, 319.) A judge's function at summary judgment is simply to decide if disputed issues of material fact make a trial necessary; the judge does not weigh the evidence or assess credibility. (Schmidt v Superior Court (2020) 44 Cal.App.5th 570, 583.) As stated in case law Respondent cites, the determination of undue influence in procuring a testamentary instrument does not depend on any one element alone, but on the "probative force" of the elements in combination. (In re Lingenfelter's Estate (1952) 38 Cal.2d 571, 585; Estate of Baker (1982) 131 Cal.App.3d 471, 481.) Lingenfelter and Baker require the beneficiary to have directly influenced procurement of the challenged testamentary instrument, rather than simply to have exercised general influence over the testator, or only had the opportunity to influence the testator (Lingenfelter 38 Cal.2d at 586; Baker 131 Cal.App.3d at 481). However, both cases allow determination of this active participation to be inferred from the facts. As put in Baker, "[a]ctivity on the part of the [alleged undue influencer] in procuring execution of the will may be established by inference, that is, by circumstantial evidence." (131 Cal.App.3d at 481.) As this Court previously stated in its February 24, 2020 Order Denying Respondent Kimberly Rhine Gagosian's Motion to Bifurcate or Sever for Early Evidentiary Hearing the Elements of Undue Benefit and Active Participation (at 2:18-24), and restates now, a finding of undue influence "need not be supported by direct evidence at the moment decedent signed the trust instruments" (Lintz v. Lintz (2014) 222 Cal.App.4th 1346, 1355), but can be proven "not only by indirect evidence but by proof of facts from which undue influence may be inferred" (Estate of Butcher (1941) 48 Cal.App.2d 465, 474). Here, Petitioner has raised disputes of fact regarding each of the elements of the undue influence claims. Specifically, Petitioner has raised factual issues regarding whether Respondent "actively procured" or "actively participated" in procuring either the 2004 Trust amendment or the 2005 Trust amendment. (See Petitioner's Separate Statement of Undisputed Facts 2 and subparts b, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, n, s.) There is no showing presented in the motion papers that Petitioner's allegations are solely "founded on surmise, suspicion and conjecture" or that Petitioner does not and cannot obtain supporting evidence for these elements of undue influence. (See, In re Kerner's Estate (1969) 275 Cal.App.2d 785, 792, and Hypertouch, Inc. v. ValueClick, Inc. (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 805, 838.) [Part 2 of 4]
